question: A process calls a CRM service then a billing service as two separate service tasks; the CRM call commits, but the billing call fails. Why do the best practices recommend treating this with a **business transaction** instead of relying on a technical ACID rollback?

options.text: ACID properties cannot be applied across distributed systems — there is no joined technical transaction spanning the workflow engine, CRM, and billing.
Zeebe automatically rolls back the CRM call once the billing call fails; "business transaction" is just a synonym for that rollback.
BPMN forbids more than one service task between gateways, so a technical transaction is structurally impossible.
gRPC is non-transactional; switching the workers to REST would let Zeebe enforce a single ACID boundary across both services.

a.text: The docs state ACID transactions cannot be applied to distributed systems; CRM, billing, and the workflow engine each have their own local transactions. A _business transaction_ accepts temporary inconsistency and resolves it explicitly.

b.text: Zeebe does not roll back already-completed service calls. A business transaction is exactly the technique used _because_ there is no automatic distributed rollback.

c.text: BPMN allows arbitrary sequences of service tasks; the constraint is the absence of distributed-ACID semantics, not BPMN structure.

d.text: The transport protocol does not give Zeebe distributed-ACID capabilities. The limitation is fundamental to distributed systems, not to gRPC vs REST.

explanation: ACID transactions apply only within a single system (typically a database). When a process orchestrates multiple services, each has its own local transactions and the workflow engine has its own — they cannot be joined into one technical transaction. A _business transaction_ marks a section that should be 'all or nothing' from a business perspective, accepting that intermediate inconsistent states may exist and must be resolved explicitly.
